explanation: This means that after prop_0 occurs, at some time in the future, prop_1 can occur (i.e., prop_1 is possible). In LTL, this is typically captured by: 'whenever prop_0 occurs, eventually prop_1 will occur'.

This expresses that if prop_0 occurs at any point, then it must be possible (i.e., it must eventually be possible) for prop_1 to occur at some time after that. This is a classical liveness property after a trigger: once the trigger happens, the effect will eventually happen.

This means that after 'prop_0' occurs, at some point in the future, 'prop_1' can occur. In LTL, 'F(prop_0 -> F prop_1)' expresses that at some point prop_0 occurs, and after that, prop_1 is possible (not required immediately, just that it's eventually possible from that point onward).

The phrase 'Once all prop_0, it shall be prop_1' can be interpreted as: once prop_0 has always held in the past (i.e., globally up to now), then prop_1 must hold from then on. In LTL, since there's no past operator, a common interpretation is: if from some point onwards prop_0 holds globally, then from that point onwards prop_1 holds globally. This can be expressed as: 'If from now on prop_0 holds globally, then from now on prop_1 holds globally.'

The given statement means that after prop_0 becomes true, prop_1 should be possible (i.e., can eventually happen). In LTL, this is expressed as: after prop_0 holds, it must be possible for prop_1 to hold eventually. Therefore, we use the formula G(prop_0 -> F(prop_1)) which ensures that every time prop_0 occurs, prop_1 will be possible (i.e., will eventually occur at some point in the future after prop_0).
This statement means that after prop_0 becomes true, prop_1 must hold forever (globally). In LTL, this is expressed using an implication with the 'globally' operator after prop_0.

The phrase means that after prop_0 has occurred at least once, there must be a path where prop_1 eventually happens. In LTL, 'it shall be possible' is translated as 'there exists a path' (which standard LTL cannot express directly, but here, means we require that after prop_0, prop_1 must be allowed to happen, i.e., there are no requirements forbidding it). Hence, we usually translate this as: 'if prop_0 ever occurs, then it is eventually possible for prop_1 to occur at some time in the future.' This is captured by: G(prop_0 -> F prop_1).

Whenever prop_0 becomes true, from that point onwards, prop_1 must hold globally. This is expressed by saying that it is always the case that if prop_0 ever becomes true, then globally (from then on), prop_1 must be true.

The requirement means that after prop_0 becomes true at least once, then at some point afterwards prop_1 must be possible (i.e., eventually true). In LTL, this is typically expressed as: If at any time prop_0 holds, then in the future, at some point, prop_1 will hold.

The statement says: 'once all prop_0, it shall be possible to prop_1.' This means that after a point where prop_0 holds globally (i.e., from then on, prop_0 is always true), from that point, it is possible for prop_1 to occur sometime in the future. To formalize this, we look for a point after which G prop_0 holds, and from that point, F prop_1 is possible. This is usually captured as: 'If from some point on always prop_0, then from that point on, eventually prop_1.' In LTL, this is written as: G( (G prop_0) -> F prop_1 ).
